title: NOC 72600 – Air pilots, flight engineers and flying instructors | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Air Pilots, Flight Engineers, and Flying Instructors (NOC 72600) are the most prioritized professionals in Canada’s immigration landscape. On February 18, 2026, the federal government officially launched a redesigned Express Entry "Transport" Category, placing NOC 72600 at the top of the list for targeted invitations. As global air travel reaches record highs and Canadian airlines expand their 2026 routes, the demand for skilled flight crews and instructors has shifted from "Moderate" to "Critical." Operating at the TEER 2 level, these professionals are the backbone of Canada’s connectivity, managing everything from transcontinental jets to specialized northern bush flights.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category7 – Trades, transport and equipment operators and related occupationsTEER Category2 (Usually requires a college diploma, apprenticeship, or significant experience)Major Group72 – Technical trades and transportation officers and controllersSub-Major Group726 – Transportation officers and controllers

duties: Pre-Flight Command: Conducting pre-flight inspections, verifying passenger/cargo distribution for weight and balance, and coordinating with ground crews.
Flight Operations: Operating aircraft safely, maintaining contact with air traffic control, and managing onboard systems in accordance with 2026 Transport Canada regulations.
Instruction (Instructors): Training student pilots in flying techniques, navigation, radio procedures, and ground-school subjects.
Technical Management (Flight Engineers): Monitoring engine performance and aircraft systems during flight and performing in-flight repairs where necessary.
Safety & Compliance: Ensuring all flights meet strict safety standards and preparing detailed flight logs and incident reports.
employment_requirements: Education: Graduation from a certified flying or aviation school is mandatory. A university degree or college diploma is often preferred by major carriers.

Licensing (Transport Canada):Commercial Pilot License (CPL): Required to fly for hire.Airline Transport Pilot License (ATPL): Mandatory for captains of large multi-engine aircraft.Flight Instructor Rating: Required to provide flight instruction.
Commercial Pilot License (CPL): Required to fly for hire.
Airline Transport Pilot License (ATPL): Mandatory for captains of large multi-engine aircraft.
Flight Instructor Rating: Required to provide flight instruction.
Experience: For international pilots, Foreign License Conversion involves a Transport Canada medical exam (Category 1), written exams (SAMRA, SARON, INRAT), and a practical flight test.
Medical Fitness: A valid Category 1 Medical Certificate issued by a Transport Canada-approved examiner is non-negotiable.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is Very Good, with "Good" prospects across all major aviation hubs due to high fleet expansion and senior retirements:

Ontario (GTA & Regional): Very Good prospects. Roughly 5,800 people work in this occupation in Ontario. In February 2026, Ontario issued targeted OINP invitations under the Employer Job Offer: Foreign Worker stream with scores as low as 50, specifically seeking pilots to support regional and cargo hubs.

British Columbia: Strongest Demand. Over 52% of BC's aviation workforce is concentrated in the Mainland/Southwest region (YVR hub). BC projects over 1,400 openings through 2035, with a median wage of $57.69/hr—one of the highest in the country.

Alberta: High Growth. Alberta’s aviation sector is booming due to increased energy sector charters and the expansion of low-cost carriers in Calgary. Median wages reach $55.00/hr, with senior captains earning over $190,000 annually.

National Trend: Over 90% of workers in this NOC are employed full-time. The 2026 market is currently defined by a "Pilot Shortage Pivot," where airlines are offering record-breaking signing bonuses and accelerated career paths for instructors.
Why Work in Canada as a Pilot or Instructor?
Elite Career Path: Progression from regional carriers to major international airlines (Air Canada, WestJet) is at its fastest rate in history.
Top-Tier Compensation: As a TEER 2 professional, your earning potential is immense, with a national high-tier wage of $96.15/hr and full 2026-grade benefit packages.
Lead Aviation Training: With a record number of new students, Flying Instructors are in high demand, offering a stable pathway into the Canadian aviation industry.
The #1 PR Priority: As of February 2026, this is one of only four occupations in the Express Entry Transport Category, allowing you to bypass high CRS scores in general draws.
pathways: Express Entry (2026 Transport Category): New for 2026! Candidates with 12 months of experience in NOC 72600 (within the last 3 years) can receive an ITA at significantly lower scores. International experience is fully recognized.

OINP Employer Job Offer: In February 2026, OINP issued invitations to candidates in NOC 72600 with a score of 50+. This is a prime route if you have a job offer from a Canadian airline or flight school.
BC PNP Skills Immigration: Pilots are a priority for BC's economy. Candidates with a job offer and a high-wage profile (typically $70+/hr) receive priority processing in 2026 draws.
2026 TR to PR Pathway: A confirmed initiative targeting up to 33,000 temporary residents. Pilots and instructors currently working in Canada are classified as "High-Priority Essential Professionals."
